exercises.title: Stress Source Mapping
exercises.description: Map your top 5 stress sources. For each, identify: (1) the trigger, (2) your physical response, (3) how long the stress lasts, and (4) one action you can take to reduce it. Create a simple table and fill it in honestly.
hints: Be specific — 'work stress' is too vague, 'unclear requirements from product team' is actionable
Notice physical responses — tension headaches, tight shoulders, insomnia are stress signals
Focus on what you CAN control, not what you can't

solution.code:
STRESS SOURCE MAP:

| Source              | Trigger              | Physical Response   | Duration    | Action                      |
|---------------------|----------------------|---------------------|-------------|----------------------------|
| Unclear requirements| Product changes spec | Jaw clenching       | Hours-days  | Ask for written specs       |
| Code review anxiety | Critical comments    | Racing heart        | 30-60 min   | Reframe as learning, not attack |
| On-call anxiety     | Pager at 2 AM        | Can't fall back     | All night   | Rotate with team, limit to 1 week/month |
| Imposter syndrome   | New technology       | Impulse to overwork | Days        | Track accomplishments file |
| Deadline pressure   | Sprint commitment    | Shallow breathing   | Weeks       | Break into milestones, flag delays early |

PRIORITY: Address #1 (unclear requirements) first — it affects daily work.

exercises.title: Design Your Stress Recovery Protocol
exercises.description: Create a personal set of quick stress recovery techniques you can use throughout the day. Include: (1) a 1-minute technique for acute stress, (2) a 5-minute technique for moderate stress, and (3) a 30-minute technique for high stress. Practice each one this week and note which works best for you.
hints: The technique should be something you can do anywhere (no special equipment needed)
Physical techniques (breathing, movement) work faster than mental techniques
Practice when you're NOT stressed so you can use them when you ARE

solution.code:
PERSONAL STRESS RECOVERY PROTOCOL:

1-MINUTE TECHNIQUE (acute stress, e.g., production incident):
Box Breathing:
• Inhale 4 counts
• Hold 4 counts  
• Exhale 4 counts
• Hold 4 counts
• Repeat 4 times
When: During high-pressure moments (debugging crisis, urgent Slack)

5-MINUTE TECHNIQUE (moderate stress, e.g., frustrating code review):
Physical Reset:
• Stand up from desk (30 sec)
• Walk to water cooler, drink water (1 min)
• 10 shoulder rolls + 10 neck rolls (1 min)
• 5 deep breaths with eyes closed (1 min)
• Write down what's bothering you in 2 sentences (1 min)
When: After frustrating interactions, before next task

30-MINUTE TECHNIQUE (high stress, e.g., bad day/week):
Stress Discharge:
• 15 min brisk walk outside (no phone)
• 10 min journaling: What happened? How do I feel? What can I control?
• 5 min: Plan one small action for tomorrow
When: End of stressful days, before evening routine

WEEK 1 TESTING:
| Day | Stress Level | Technique Used | Effectiveness |
|-----|-------------|----------------|---------------|
| Mon | 7/10        | Box breathing   | 6/10          |
| Tue | 5/10        | Physical reset  | 8/10          |
| Wed | 8/10        | Walk + journal  | 9/10          |
| Thu | 6/10        | Box breathing   | 7/10          |
| Fri | 9/10        | Walk + journal  | 8/10          |

FINDING: Physical reset (5-min) works best for moderate stress. Journaling works best for high stress.

exercises.title: Weekly Stress Audit
exercises.description: At the end of each day this week, rate your stress level (1-10) and note: what caused it, what helped, and what you'd do differently. At the end of the week, review the patterns and create a 'stress prevention plan' for next week.
hints: Be honest with ratings — hiding stress from yourself doesn't help
Look for patterns: are certain days or activities consistently stressful?
The goal isn't zero stress — it's managing stress before it becomes chronic

solution.code:
WEEKLY STRESS AUDIT:

| Day  | Stress | Cause              | What Helped        | Do Differently           |
|------|--------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Mon  | 6/10   | Sprint planning    | Morning exercise   | Prep questions before    |
| Tue  | 4/10   | Normal day         | Deep work blocks   | Nothing — good day       |
| Wed  | 7/10   | Code review conflict| Walk outside     | Address issues earlier   |
| Thu  | 8/10   | Production bug     | Box breathing      | Don't skip lunch         |
| Fri  | 5/10   | Week winding down  | Shutdown ritual    | Start earlier            |

WEEKLY PATTERN ANALYSIS:
• Average stress: 6.0/10
• Highest: Thursday (production bug)
• Lowest: Tuesday (normal day)
• Most effective intervention: Morning exercise (reduced baseline by 2 points)
• Recurring trigger: Code review conflicts (addressed Wed)

STRESS PREVENTION PLAN FOR NEXT WEEK:
1. Continue morning exercise (reduces baseline stress)
2. Schedule buffer time before sprint planning (prep reduces anxiety)
3. Address code review concerns in real-time, not after the fact
4. Never skip lunch during high-stress days (blood sugar affects stress)
5. Keep shutdown ritual to prevent weekend stress carryover
